draft.introduction: Martial’s Epigrams Book 1 introduces the genre he perfected: short, witty, often savage poems about Roman life. His preface defends the explicit humor of his verse while his poems dissect social pretension with surgical precision.

translation.text: I hope that in my little books I have observed such restraint that whoever has a fair mind cannot complain of them. They do not attack persons but spare them, which was not the custom of the ancients. I trust my pages will not be interpreted by the malicious. It is wrong to exercise one’s cleverness upon another’s book.
translation.original_text: Spero me secutum in libellis meis tale temperamentum ut de illis queri non possit quisquis de se bene senserit

translator_notes.note: Martial’s defense of literary freedom—“my page is wanton but my life is moral”—established a distinction between author and persona that remained central to Western literary theory. His epigrammatic form influenced the entire European tradition of wit from Jonson through Wilde.
